pattern: Walk each same-value region while remembering only the one cell you just came from; meeting an already-visited same-value cell that isn't that immediate predecessor proves a loop exists.
hints: Explore the grid region by region, only ever moving into a neighbor that shares the current cell's value.
Track visited cells per region and remember the single cell you just stepped from, since stepping straight back to it is not a cycle.

If a same-value neighbor was already visited and it is not the cell you just came from, you have closed a loop of length four or more — report true immediately, otherwise finish exploring all regions before returning false.

edge_inputs.description: single cell, nothing to loop through
smallest possible loop, a 2x2 block of one value
single row, a straight line can never close on itself
single column, same reasoning as a row
a plus-shaped region has a branch point but is a tree, not a cycle
same value only touches diagonally, which is never a valid move, so no path connects them at all
a ring surrounding a differently-valued center closes a genuine cycle
several separate regions, only one of which happens to contain a cycle

stress.description: modest random grid over a 3-letter alphabet, dense enough to frequently form cycles
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import math
    dim = max(2, min(40, math.isqrt(max(1, size))))
    alphabet = ['a', 'b', 'c']
    grid = [[rng.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(dim)] for _ in range(dim)]
    return [grid]

stress.description: larger near-maximum-size grid over only 2 letters, to exercise scale without slowing the oracle
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import math
    dim = max(2, min(300, math.isqrt(max(1, size))))
    alphabet = ['a', 'b']
    grid = [[rng.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(dim)] for _ in range(dim)]
    return [grid]
stress.note: only 2 letters keeps regions large so cycles show up often at this scale
